title: Prefix Sum
description: Precompute once, answer range-sum queries in O(1): the classic prefix sum technique.
objectives: Understand the problem prefix sums solve
Build a prefix sum array in O(n)
Answer any range-sum query in O(1) using it
Know the exact formula and why it works

sections.text: 1. The Problem: Repeated Range Sum Queries
If a range-sum question gets asked many times on the same array, recomputing the sum by looping every single time wastes a lot of repeated work.
A prefix sum array stores, at each index i, the running total of all elements from the start up to and including i — enabling any range sum to be computed in O(1) afterward.
prefix[i] always means "the sum of everything from index 0 through index i." prefix[3] = 14 means numbers[0] + numbers[1] + numbers[2] + numbers[3] = 2+4+1+7 = 14.

sections.code:
// sum of indices l through r (inclusive):
int rangeSum = (l == 0) ? prefix[r] : prefix[r] - prefix[l - 1];

sections.text: Sum from l to r = (sum from 0 to r) minus (sum from 0 to l-1) — subtracting away everything before l leaves exactly the range wanted. This is a single subtraction, O(1), no matter how large the range is.

sections.title: Verifying the answer
points: prefix[4] - prefix[0] = 17 - 2 = 15 — same answer, computed without touching those middle elements directly

sections.text: 6. Complexity: O(n) Build, O(1) Per Query

This is a textbook time-space tradeoff (recall Space Complexity): spending O(n) extra space and O(n) upfront time in exchange for every future query becoming instant. Worth it whenever many range-sum queries will be made on the same, unchanging array.

sections.title: A bank statement's running balance column
sections.text: A bank statement doesn't force you to re-add every single transaction to find your balance on a given day — it shows a running total after each transaction. Finding "how much did I spend between March 3rd and March 10th" is just subtracting one running total from another, instead of re-adding every transaction in between.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting the special case when l == 0 — there's no prefix[-1] to subtract, so the range sum is just prefix[r] directly.
Off-by-one errors in the range formula — double check whether the range is meant to be inclusive or exclusive of r.
Rebuilding the prefix sum array for every single query — it should be built once, up front, and reused for all queries.

Using prefix sum when the underlying array changes frequently — recomputing the whole prefix array after every update defeats the technique's benefit; that scenario calls for a different structure entirely.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What problem does the prefix sum technique solve?
questions.answer: Answering many range-sum queries on the same array efficiently, avoiding the O(n) cost of re-summing a range from scratch every time.

questions.question: What does prefix[i] represent?
questions.answer: The sum of all elements from index 0 through index i, inclusive.

questions.question: How do you compute the sum of elements from index l to r using a prefix sum array?
questions.answer: prefix[r] - prefix[l - 1], with the special case that if l is 0, the answer is simply prefix[r].

questions.question: What is the time complexity of building the prefix sum array, and of each query afterward?
questions.answer: O(n) to build it once, and O(1) per query afterward.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: Prefix sum precomputes, in O(n), a running total array where prefix[i] holds the sum of all elements from index 0 through i. Once built, any range sum from index l to r can be answered in O(1) with prefix[r] - prefix[l - 1] (or just prefix[r] when l is 0), since subtracting away everything before l leaves exactly the desired range. This trades O(n) upfront time and space for turning every future range-sum query into a single subtraction, making it ideal whenever many range queries are made against the same unchanging array.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Prefix Sum
sections.text: prefix[i] = running total through i, like a bank statement's balance column. Range sum = prefix[r] - prefix[l-1]. Subtract away what came before.
